fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we've been getting complaints from backend devs that wiring up our reactive DB pooling layer is still way too manual — people keep misconfiguring timeouts, getting pool size mismatches, or just not knowing which driver gets picked up automatically. We need a library that lets devs just drop in a connection string (or use a step-by-step builder in code) and have everything figured out for them — sizes, timeouts, which underlying driver to use, all of it. No more hand-rolling type conversions or forgetting to validate that max pool size isn't smaller than the starting size.

The tricky part is we want this to work like the compatibility logic from that driver-registration pattern we used in the auth/datasource module — where the provider only claims ownership of requests that actually belong to it. Also, there's a specific sentinel value for 'no timeout set' that the old team agreed on internally — check with whoever wrote the duration handling utils, I don't have the exact string handy.

Errors should come back as plain category labels, not stack traces or technical messages. The output format for configs needs to be consistent and always in the same field order. We also need raw values (strings, numbers, mixed types coming in from URL params) to be coerced automatically. Scope includes URL-based config, programmatic builder, type coercion, and auto driver discovery wrapping. Please make sure JMX naming edge cases are handled — there were bugs there before.

One follow-up from the questions that came in: for the old "no timeout configured" behavior, the sentinel is `PT-0.001S`. That's the value we should render for maxCreateConnectionTime, maxAcquireTime, maxLifeTime, and backgroundEvictionInterval whenever those are not set. Also, the rendered config needs to be totally predictable and always include every field in this exact order: initialSize, maxSize, acquireRetry, maxIdleTime, maxCreateConnectionTime, maxAcquireTime, maxLifeTime, backgroundEvictionInterval, validationDepth, validationQuery, name, registerJmx. Even if something wasn't explicitly provided, it still shows up in output.

A couple defaults to lock in as well: initialSize and maxSize both default to 10, acquireRetry defaults to 1, maxIdleTime defaults to PT30M, validationDepth defaults to LOCAL, and registerJmx defaults to false. validationQuery, name are absent by default and should render as `<none>`. For the pool naming piece, the connection URL query parameter is `poolName`, like `?poolName=requiredHere`, and that maps to the `name` field in rendered output. If `poolName` isn't there, name should be `<none>`.

On the sizing behavior, if only maxSize is provided, then initialSize stays at 10 when maxSize >= 10, but if maxSize < 10 then initialSize should be lowered to match maxSize. The flip side is the same idea: if only initialSize is provided and initialSize > 10, then maxSize gets raised to equal initialSize; otherwise maxSize stays at 10. That should cover the mismatch cases people keep tripping over.

And for the driver ownership piece, the fixed pooling driver id is `pool`. It only handles a request when the driver selector exactly equals `pool`. If the selector is missing, or it's anything else like `mock`, then it's handled=false. The response should always include driver=<value or <none>>, handled=true/false, and poolingDriver=pool. This is the same behavior we want in that driver-identification unit, including the `handles` check returning true only for the exact string `pool`.

trigger_keywords: what validation errors can the builder throw
what are the error categories for invalid builder configurations
when does building a config fail and what error label is emitted
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The builder emits exactly one of these normalized error category labels: `connection_factory_required` when no delegate connection factory is supplied; `jmx_name_required` when registerJmx is true but no pool name is set; `max_size_below_initial_size` when the explicitly requested maxSize is less than the explicitly requested initialSize. Each failure renders as a single line: `error=<category>`.

trigger_keywords: how does the URL parser extract the delegate driver name
what is the expected shape of a pooling connection URL
how is the nested protocol parsed from a pool URL
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A pooling connection URL has the shape `r2dbc:pool:<delegate>://host?<params>`. The segment between `pool:` and `://` is the delegate driver name (e.g. `mock`). Query parameters map to pool settings. The URL parser must extract the delegate name and all recognized query parameters to build the configuration.

trigger_keywords: how does validationDepth coercion work
is the enum match case sensitive
how are string enum values normalized for validation depth
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The validationDepth parameter (from URL or coerce op) is matched case-insensitively. Both `remote` and `REMOTE` resolve to the canonical value `REMOTE`. Both `local` and `LOCAL` resolve to `LOCAL`. The canonical output is always uppercase.

trigger_keywords: what happens when an option binding fails to coerce the value
what error is returned for an unconvertible option value
how is a conversion failure reported during option binding
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a named option is present but its value cannot be coerced (e.g. `abc` as an integer), the binding operation fails with two lines: `error=option_conversion_failed` followed by `option=<key-name>`. When the option is simply absent, nothing is bound and the output is `bound=false`. When present and coercible, output is `bound=true` followed by `value=<coerced-value>`.

trigger_keywords: how does delegate discovery work
what does the discover operation return
how is the wrapped pooling factory verified to preserve delegate identity
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `discover` operation resolves a pooling URL, finds the delegate driver named in the nested protocol, wraps it, and verifies identity preservation. Output must include: `delegateDriver=<name>` (e.g. `mock`), `unwrapsToDelegate=true` (confirming the wrapper unwraps back to the original delegate factory), and `metadataMatchesDelegate=true` (confirming the wrapper exposes the delegate's metadata unchanged).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'driver', 'url-config', 'build-config', 'coerce', 'bind', 'discover'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: check with whoever wrote the duration handling utils
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The value-coercion/option-mapping unit responsible for parsing ISO-8601 duration strings and rendering them — Feature 4.3 duration coercion and Feature 2 URL duration parameters

context_pointers.vague_reference: JMX naming edge cases that had bugs before
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The builder validation constraint: when registerJmx=true but no pool name (poolName / name) is provided, the build must fail with error=jmx_name_required — Feature 3.3 constraint validation

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have the specific details on that handy — you'll want to dig into the existing codebase or check the internal design notes. The key thing from a product perspective is that configs should always be fully resolved with consistent output, errors should be human-readable category labels (not raw exceptions), and the driver selection logic needs to be self-contained so it only claims requests that actually belong to the pooling layer.

feature1_driver_identification.json.description: Driver identification: the pooling provider advertises a stable driver identifier and decides whether it handles a given driver selector. It handles only its own pooling selector and declines requests with a missing or foreign driver selector.

feature2_url_defaults.json.description: Connection-URL to resolved configuration: parse a pooling connection URL whose nested protocol names a delegate driver, then resolve the full effective pool configuration. With no query parameters all pool settings fall back to their defaults. Timeouts that are not configured render as the sentinel negative duration meaning 'no timeout'.

cases.expected_output:
initialSize=10
maxSize=10
acquireRetry=1
maxIdleTime=PT30M
maxCreateConnectionTime=PT-0.001S
maxAcquireTime=PT-0.001S
maxLifeTime=PT-0.001S
backgroundEvictionInterval=PT-0.001S
validationDepth=LOCAL
validationQuery=<none>
name=<none>
registerJmx=false

feature2_url_durations.json.description: ISO-8601 duration parameters from the connection URL: each timeout query parameter (maxLifeTime, maxAcquireTime, maxIdleTime, maxCreateConnectionTime, backgroundEvictionInterval) is parsed from its ISO-8601 text and applied independently to the matching configuration field, leaving the other timeouts at their default sentinels.

maxIdleTime=PT30M

maxAcquireTime=PT-0.001S
maxLifeTime=PT30M
backgroundEvictionInterval=PT-0.001S
validationDepth=LOCAL
validationQuery=<none>
name=<none>
registerJmx=false

feature2_url_sizing.json.description: Sizing parameters from the connection URL: initialSize and maxSize query parameters are parsed to integers and applied to the resolved configuration, overriding the defaults. acquireRetry is likewise parsed to an integer.

feature3_1_builder_explicit.json.description: Programmatic configuration builder with explicit settings: starting from a valid delegate connection factory, the builder accepts an explicit mix of settings (validation query, idle timeout, initial and max size, pool name, JMX flag) and produces a configuration whose fields reflect exactly those settings while unset fields keep their defaults.

cases.expected_output:
initialSize=2
maxSize=20
acquireRetry=1
maxIdleTime=PT1S
maxCreateConnectionTime=PT-0.001S
maxAcquireTime=PT-0.001S
maxLifeTime=PT-0.001S
backgroundEvictionInterval=PT-0.001S
validationDepth=LOCAL
validationQuery=foo
name=bar
registerJmx=true

feature3_2_builder_sizing_defaults.json.description: Pool sizing defaults and reconciliation in the programmatic builder: when only one of initial size / max size is specified, the other is derived. Specifying only max size keeps initial size at the default of 10 when max is at least 10, otherwise lowers initial size to match max. Specifying only initial size raises max size to equal initial size when it exceeds the default of 10.

cases.expected_output:
initialSize=10
maxSize=20
acquireRetry=1
maxIdleTime=PT30M
maxCreateConnectionTime=PT-0.001S
maxAcquireTime=PT-0.001S
maxLifeTime=PT-0.001S
backgroundEvictionInterval=PT-0.001S
validationDepth=LOCAL
validationQuery=<none>
name=<none>
registerJmx=false

initialSize=5
maxSize=5
acquireRetry=1
maxIdleTime=PT30M
maxCreateConnectionTime=PT-0.001S
maxAcquireTime=PT-0.001S
maxLifeTime=PT-0.001S
backgroundEvictionInterval=PT-0.001S
validationDepth=LOCAL
validationQuery=<none>
name=<none>
registerJmx=false

feature3_3_builder_validation_errors.jso.description: Validation of configuration constraints in the programmatic builder. Building fails with a neutral error category when: no delegate connection factory is supplied; JMX registration is enabled without a pool name; or the requested max size is below the requested initial size. Each failure is reported as a single normalized error line.

feature4_1_coerce_integer.json.description: Integer coercion: a configuration value is coerced to an integer. Numeric inputs (whether integral or fractional in representation) and numeric strings all coerce to the same integer value by truncation/parsing.

feature4_2_coerce_enum.json.description: Validation-depth enum coercion: a configuration value is coerced to a validation-depth category. A string is matched case-insensitively to the category name, so both lower-case and upper-case text resolve to the same canonical category.

feature4_3_coerce_duration.json.description: Duration coercion: a configuration value is coerced to a duration. An ISO-8601 duration string is parsed into the equivalent duration value, rendered back in canonical ISO-8601 form.

feature4_4_option_binding.json.description: Conditional option binding: an option source binds a value to a consumer only when the option is present, applying a coercion in between. When the named option is present its coerced value is delivered (bound=true). When the option is absent the consumer is never invoked (bound=false). When the option is present but its value cannot be coerced, binding fails with a neutral conversion-failure category naming the option.

feature5_delegate_discovery.json.description: Delegate discovery and identity preservation: resolving a pooling connection URL discovers the delegate driver named by the nested protocol, wraps it in a pooling factory, and preserves the delegate's identity. The wrapper unwraps back to the exact delegate factory and reports the delegate's metadata unchanged.
